summary: analysis-chart ships an install-time Windows DLL dropper with scheduled-task persistence
details: On `npm install`, the package's `install` lifecycle script runs `lib/chart-loader.js`, which fetches an encrypted blob from https://github.com/Analysis-Chart/analysis-chart/releases/download/v1/payload.bin.enc, XOR-decrypts it byte-by-byte with single-byte key 0x42 (`Buffer.from('Qg==','base64')[0]`), base64-decodes the result to a Windows PE/DLL (validated via MZ header), writes it to %APPDATA%\Roaming\Microsoft\Windows\<random>.dll, and executes it via `spawn('rundll32.exe', [dllPath, 'Run'],...)`. The loader then runs `schtasks /change /tn "WindowsUpdateService" /delay 0001:00` to establish persistence under a Microsoft-impersonating task name, and finally spawns a detached `node -e` cleanup that deletes the package directory and removes the `analysis-chart` entry from the parent project's package.json to erase forensic traces. The package's `index.js` exports a benign-looking statistics/charts API (`stats`, `outliers`, `trend`, `correlation`, `movingAverage`, `analyze`) as cover so `require('analysis-chart')` appears legitimate after the install-time compromise. The layered XOR+base64 encoding of the fetched DLL has no benign purpose for a charting library and is intended for scanner evasion.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
